1	---
2	name: chemical-entity
3	description: For working with any terms referencing chemical entities (CHEBI). CHEBI has some unusual properties that are counter-intuitive, so always use this skill to plan or check work that involves a chemical entity or CHEBI IDs, whether a new term, or significant alteration of existing terms
4	---
5	
6	# When to use
7	
8	- Find ChEBI IDs for chemical entities to use in GO term definitions
9	- Determine the correct protonation state for a chemical
10	- Understand chemical classification and parent classes
11	- Create biosynthetic process or metabolic process terms that reference chemicals
12	- Verify chemical cross-references
13	
14	The general thing to keep in mind is that selecting the right CHEBI term is harder than it seems. Don't assume that the term with the most biologist-friendly label is the right one!
15	
16	CRITICAL: Protonation State Selection (pH 7.3 Canonicalization)
17	
18	**For GO term logical definitions, ALWAYS prefer the major species at physiological pH (approximately 7.3).**
19	
20	- for chemicals with different protonation states, the ph7.3 form must be used
21	- for chemicals with different stereoisomers, the appropriate L or D form must be used (except in very rare cases where we want to be agnostic)
22	
23	## Exemplar term
24	
25	```
26	id: GO:0005290
27	name: L-histidine transmembrane transporter activity
28	namespace: molecular_function
29	def: "Enables the transfer of L-histidine from one side of a membrane to the other. L-histidine is 2-amino-3-(1H-imidazol-4-yl)propanoic acid." [GOC:ai, GOC:mtg_transport, ISBN:0815340729]
30	synonym: "histidine/arginine/lysine/ornithine porter activity" NARROW []
31	synonym: "L-histidine transporter activity" BROAD []
32	is_a: GO:0015174 ! basic amino acid transmembrane transporter activity
33	intersection_of: GO:0022857 ! transmembrane transporter activity
34	intersection_of: has_primary_input CHEBI:57595 ! L-histidine zwitterion
35	relationship: part_of GO:0089709 ! L-histidine transmembrane transport
36	```
37	
38	Corresponds to `src/design_patterns/transmembrane_transport.yaml` design pattern. Note that even if the user requested `histidine` we would have interpreted that to mean the L-form. Note also that there is a CHEBI term with the label "L-histidine" (CHEBI:15971), but this is not the ph7.3 form, we instead use the ph7.3 form. However, we retain the biologist friendly label.
39	
40	## Core Tool: OAK (Ontology Access Kit)
41	
42	### Basic ChEBI Lookup
43	
44	The primary tool for ChEBI lookups is OAK with the sqlite adapter:
45	
46	```bash
47	# Search by name (exact match)
48	runoak -i sqlite:obo:chebi info 'L-cysteine'
49	
50	# Search by fuzzy match (any match)
51	runoak -i sqlite:obo:chebi info 'l~terpenoid'
52	
53	# Search by prefix (starts with)
54	runoak -i sqlite:obo:chebi info 'l^glutam'
55	# Finds terms starting with "glutam"
56	```
57	
58	## 7.3 mapping
59	
60	Here is how we would obtain the correct term for L-histidine
61	
62	```
63	runoak -i sqlite:obo:chebi info L-histidine
64	```
65	==> CHEBI:15971
66	
67	
68	Look this up
69	
70	```
71	cd src/ontology && wget ftp://ftp.expasy.org/databases/rhea/tsv/chebi_pH7_3_mapping.tsv
72	grep '^15971\t' chebi_pH7_3_mapping.tsv
73	```
74	
75	yields
76	
77	```
78	15971   57595   computation
79	```
80	
81	Check:
82	
83	```
84	runoak -i sqlite:obo:chebi info CHEBI:57595
85	```
86	
87	==> `CHEBI:57595 ! L-histidine zwitterion`
88

file.content:
---
name: chemical-entity
description: For working with any terms referencing chemical entities (CHEBI). CHEBI has some unusual properties that are counter-intuitive, so always use this skill to plan or check work that involves a chemical entity or CHEBI IDs, whether a new term, or significant alteration of existing terms
---

# When to use

- Find ChEBI IDs for chemical entities to use in GO term definitions
- Determine the correct protonation state for a chemical
- Understand chemical classification and parent classes
- Create biosynthetic process or metabolic process terms that reference chemicals
- Verify chemical cross-references

The general thing to keep in mind is that selecting the right CHEBI term is harder than it seems. Don't assume that the term with the most biologist-friendly label is the right one!

CRITICAL: Protonation State Selection (pH 7.3 Canonicalization)

**For GO term logical definitions, ALWAYS prefer the major species at physiological pH (approximately 7.3).**

- for chemicals with different protonation states, the ph7.3 form must be used
- for chemicals with different stereoisomers, the appropriate L or D form must be used (except in very rare cases where we want to be agnostic)

## Exemplar term

```
id: GO:0005290
name: L-histidine transmembrane transporter activity
namespace: molecular_function
def: "Enables the transfer of L-histidine from one side of a membrane to the other. L-histidine is 2-amino-3-(1H-imidazol-4-yl)propanoic acid." [GOC:ai, GOC:mtg_transport, ISBN:0815340729]
synonym: "histidine/arginine/lysine/ornithine porter activity" NARROW []
synonym: "L-histidine transporter activity" BROAD []
is_a: GO:0015174 ! basic amino acid transmembrane transporter activity
intersection_of: GO:0022857 ! transmembrane transporter activity
intersection_of: has_primary_input CHEBI:57595 ! L-histidine zwitterion
relationship: part_of GO:0089709 ! L-histidine transmembrane transport
```

Corresponds to `src/design_patterns/transmembrane_transport.yaml` design pattern. Note that even if the user requested `histidine` we would have interpreted that to mean the L-form. Note also that there is a CHEBI term with the label "L-histidine" (CHEBI:15971), but this is not the ph7.3 form, we instead use the ph7.3 form. However, we retain the biologist friendly label.

## Core Tool: OAK (Ontology Access Kit)

### Basic ChEBI Lookup

The primary tool for ChEBI lookups is OAK with the sqlite adapter:

```bash
# Search by name (exact match)
runoak -i sqlite:obo:chebi info 'L-cysteine'

# Search by fuzzy match (any match)
runoak -i sqlite:obo:chebi info 'l~terpenoid'

# Search by prefix (starts with)
runoak -i sqlite:obo:chebi info 'l^glutam'
# Finds terms starting with "glutam"
```

## 7.3 mapping

Here is how we would obtain the correct term for L-histidine

```
runoak -i sqlite:obo:chebi info L-histidine
```
==> CHEBI:15971


Look this up

```
cd src/ontology && wget ftp://ftp.expasy.org/databases/rhea/tsv/chebi_pH7_3_mapping.tsv
grep '^15971\t' chebi_pH7_3_mapping.tsv
```

yields

```
15971   57595   computation
```

Check:

```
runoak -i sqlite:obo:chebi info CHEBI:57595
```

==> `CHEBI:57595 ! L-histidine zwitterion`

## Summary

Per @ValWood's request in #31114, this PR renames two existing terms to use the originally-requested biologist-friendly labels:

- **GO:0180067**: `terreate biosynthetic process` → `terreic acid biosynthetic process`
- **GO:0180069**: `positive regulation of terreate biosynthetic process` → `positive regulation of terreic acid biosynthetic process`

## Context

The terms were originally requested in #31114 with the labels `terreic acid biosynthetic process` and `positive regulation of terreic acid biosynthetic process`. They were added in PR #31612 but used `terreate` (the pH 7.3 form) as the primary label and `terreic acid` as a related synonym. The previous attempt to rename was in PR #31374 which was closed due to ID conflicts.

This rename brings the labels in line with the **GO chemical-entity convention**, which uses biologist-friendly labels in term names while retaining the pH 7.3 form CHEBI ID (here `CHEBI:233617 ! terreate`) in the logical definition. See the exemplar in `.claude/skills/chemical-entity/SKILL.md`: `L-histidine transmembrane transporter activity` uses the biologist label even though the logical definition references `CHEBI:57595 ! L-histidine zwitterion`.

## Changes

- Labels swapped (`terreate` → `terreic acid` in both term names).
- Synonyms swapped: `terreate biosynthetic process` and `positive regulation of terreate biosynthetic process` are now RELATED synonyms.
- Logical definitions and asserted is_a relationships unchanged. The pH 7.3 form `CHEBI:233617 ! terreate` remains the chemical referenced in the logical def.
- Definition of GO:0180069 brought into line with the standard 'positive regulation' phrasing (`"Any process that activates or increases the frequency, rate or extent of terreic acid biosynthetic process."`).
- Definitions still cite PMID:25265334.
- `created_by`, `creation_date`, `term_tracker_item` left untouched (these are existing terms).

- [x] REFERENCE-VALIDATION: PMID:25265334 already in the ontology, unchanged
- [x] CHANGES-COMMITTED
